data.title: Europe's AI U-Turn Raises Doubts, Hopes
data.description:
As the EU mobilizes a €200 billion investment designed to spur new AI datacenters and supercomputers, experts warn that it will take more than funding for Europe to catch up in the global AI race.

In April, the European Commission outlined the details of its AI Continent Action Plan, which aims to make the EU a "global leader in AI." The plan calls for the construction of AI factories and gigafactories, aiming to triple the EU's datacenter capacity in the next five to seven years. Under the plan, the EU wants to increase access to high-quality data for training AI models.

Coupled with deregulatory efforts, this strategy marks a significant shift for a region traditionally skeptical of technology companies and inclined toward regulation. Given that long history, experts say the EU's AI efforts will require more than just spending. More profound reforms — including immigration policies and power generation — may be required to enhance Europe's competitiveness.

"There is an entire civil service structure that requires reform, and it needs to be empowered by innovation," said Alexandru Voica, head of corporate affairs and policy at Synthesia Ltd., a leading European AI startup. "You need to send signals from the top and encourage people to experiment with new technologies."

AI gigafactories

In building the AI infrastructure, the EU aims to mirror the success of past government initiatives such as the European Organization for Nuclear Research, an intergovernmental organization operating the largest particle physics laboratory in the world.

"This unique public-private partnership, akin to a CERN [European Organization for Nuclear Research] for AI, will enable all our scientists and companies - not just the biggest - to develop the most advanced very large models needed to make Europe an AI continent,” European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen said in the press release announcing the €200 billion investment. The EU will allocate €20 billion, with the remainder coming from private investors.

The EU plans to establish five "AI gigafactories," each anticipated to house about 100,000 state-of-the-art AI chips like those from Nvidia Corp. This is four times more than the current seven AI factories that are being built in countries including Italy, Germany and Spain. All told, the EU will have 13 supercomputers for AI companies and computer scientists to use.

Thomas Regnier, European Commission spokesperson for tech sovereignty, defence, space and research, told S&P Global Market Intelligence that access to the AI infrastructure will be free-of-charge for startups that use it for "innovation purposes." Commercial access will be allowed on a pay-per-use basis.

"AI Factories will bring together the key ingredients that are needed for success in AI: computing power, data and talent," Regnier said.

This time is different

Unlike nuclear research, AI development is moving at breakneck speed. This could present a challenge for European consortia that include collaborative groups formed by EU member states, research institutions and technology hubs.

"Consortia are very good at getting money, but not very good at achieving results," Bert Hubert, the founder of network solutions company PowerDNS.COM BV and a former regulator in the Netherlands, said in an interview with Market Intelligence.

For every European Organization for Nuclear Research success story, there is a Gaia-X. Gaia-X is an initiative to create a "cloud ecosystem," believed to be the European answer to US hyperscalers, Microsoft Corp.'s Azure and Alphabet Inc.'s Google Cloud Platform.

Gaia-X has failed to deliver meaningful results so far. Experts still struggle to understand what the initiative does exactly. The organization repeatedly denied that it is a European hyperscaler, claiming instead that it aims to create a data-sharing environment. Hubert has called for the Gaia-X project to be abandoned, and he is not alone.

"Gaia-X has been an unmitigated disaster," said Johan Vermij, an analyst at Market Intelligence 451 Research.

The fear now is that history will repeat itself with the AI initiative. AI technology is developing much faster than previous technology shifts like mobile and software as a service. And US cloud hyperscalers have a software advantage that is not easy to replicate. On the public cloud, developers can gain quick access to all the chips and software necessary to build AI applications.

Synthesia, a GenAI video generator for enterprise use cases that recently closed a $180 million funding round at a post-money valuation of $2.1 billion, is building its applications on Amazon.com Inc.'s (AMZN) Amazon Web Services and Google Cloud Platform.

"The challenge you have with [EU] supercomputers is that they have specific software stacks. If you build commercial applications in the hyperscalers' cloud, it's much easier to move software workloads, while supercomputers get bottlenecked," Voica said.

The gigafactories can be more useful in solving deep research problems, such as drug discovery, which are not subject to commercial competition. Mistral AI SAS, the leading European provider of foundation models, built its early foundation model, Mistral 7B, on Leonardo, an EU supercomputer in Italy. Mistral complained about the lack of computing power in Europe. It is now reportedly building its own cloud infrastructure. Mistral did not reply to a request for comment.

Synthesia runs its workloads on hyperscalers' datacenters in Europe, but sometimes it needs to tap into the US datacenters due to a lack of capacity on the continent.

European GenAI startups

Market Intelligence counted more than 130 GenAI startups based in Europe. About a third of those are in the UK, and another third in France and Germany combined. Together, these companies have raised a total of more than $5 billion in venture capital since inception, according to Market Intelligence. "There are a couple of companies that are promising, but without government backing, they cannot compete with the US companies," Vermij said.

The EU numbers pale in comparison to the US, which has three times as many GenAI startups that have collectively raised $161 billion. Large multibillion-dollar funding rounds in the US have been closed by leading GenAI foundation model makers such as OpenAI LLC, Anthropic PBC and X.AI Corp.

Changed approach

Experts say the EU would need to make reforms that have little to do with technology itself to boost the GenAI startup scene. Specifically, the region needs more talent and datacenters.

To attract talent, startups say they need a better visa regime. An article by European computer scientists Nuria Oliver, Bernhard Schölkopf and Yann LeCun criticized the EU's approach as project-based rather than person-centered.

"Europe's most successful scientific organizations and programs … believe that the key to groundbreaking innovations lies in identifying and attracting the brightest minds," the trio wrote. "But in the EU, this principle is in danger."

To build more datacenters, a key constraint is the energy power and regulatory requirements. The Netherlands, for instance, imposed a moratorium on new datacenter construction due to a lack of energy. Germany imposed various restrictions on new and operating datacenters as part of the country's ambitious decarbonization goals.

"The EU needs to create the underlying infrastructure, such as the energy grid that allows datacenters to exist and scale," Synthesia's Voica said.

According to 451 Research's Datacenter KnowledgeBase, the European leased datacenter footprint is half that of the US and China.

Data demand

AI companies are also complaining about the lack of European data to train their models. According to Voica, this is where the EU could establish a modern and simplified regulatory framework that would encourage content creators to share data with AI companies without fearing their data will be misused.

Synthesia had to create its own training data by setting up a studio and hiring actors to talk on camera, largely because it was hard to source third-party data. The company recently signed a contract with US-based video and photo library Shutterstock, under which it will only pay for content it chooses to train on.

The EU was the first major jurisdiction to implement regulation of AI technologies under the EU AI Act. The European Commission is now looking to simplify the rules, with AI policy unit chief Kilian Gross recently expressing openness to "targeted changes," Politico reported.

Too much regulation often has unintended consequences. Instead of leveling the playing field between large corporations and small startups, it can advantage deeper-pocketed Big Tech companies who have the resources to hire compliance officers and to fight in courts, if necessary.

"Success will rely heavily on whether the EU can sustain its current momentum politically, especially when translating ambitious plans into concrete actions, particularly through clear policy signals and strategic communication that can attract and retain investment," Öykü Özfırat, a senior policy analyst at Access Partnership, told Market Intelligence.

France taking lead

Separate governments in the EU are doing more. France, once a skeptic of datacenter construction, has now taken the lead in Europe. According to 451's Datacenter KnowledgeBaseGrowth, datacenter capacity growth in France is expected to rise 16% in 2025, the second-highest rate of growth after Ireland.

Mistral is building a massive 1.4 GW power datacenter cluster in Paris with the help of the French government and the United Arab Emirates' sovereign wealth fund MGX, in partnership with Nvidia (NVDA). It follows the footsteps of its American competitor OpenAI, which is building its 1.2 GW cluster in Texas to reduce reliance on its hyperscaler partner Microsoft.

"Given the high-energy demands of datacenters, France is well positioned to take the lead thanks to its extensive nuclear power infrastructure, which can provide a carbon-free energy source for AI datacenters," Vermij said.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Petrobras' Recent Weakness Means Opportunity
data.description:
Petróleo Brasileiro S.A. - Petrobras (NYSE:PBR) is one of the largest oil companies in the world with a $75 billion valuation. With weak oil prices, the company has underperformed in the market since our last article. The company, as the state oil company of Brazil, has a strong integrated portfolio, but has had consistent governance concerns. Despite that, Petrobras is a valuable long-term investment opportunity at its current price.

Petrobras Operational Results

Petrobras has managed to increase production to almost 2.8 million barrels / day, mid-single-digit growth over the prior quarter.

The company has continued to start up new assets with Búzios, and the company's new FPSO (#7) will produce up to 225 thousand barrels / day and process a massive 12 million cubic meters of natural gas per day. The company has had a new FPSO arrive, and it's continuing to find new discoveries that will support future production.

Petrobras Financial Results

Financially, the net result for this company has been strong performance versus its $70 billion market cap.

The company managed to earn a massive $4.5 billion in FCF for the quarter, up 20% QoQ, with $10.7 billion in EBITDA. The company has announced more than $2 USD in declared dividends, a strong double-digit dividend yield that shows what the company can comfortably afford along with its continued commitments to shareholders.

The company's adjusted EBITDA declined YoY, but the company was impacted by average Brent prices declining from $83 / barrel to $75 / barrel.

Petrobras Investments

The company is continuing to invest dramatically in growth.

The company invested $1 billion in FPSO in the quarter, with FPSOs physical progress improving by 25%. The company has a number of FPSOs under construction, which has led to increased well completion and connections. These investments are key to multi-decade reliable low-cost production from the company's unconventional assets.

The company is continuing to invest dramatically with $16 billion in annual capex. That's more than 20% of the company's market capitalization, which shows how heavily the company is investing versus its valuation. That will enable the company to continue growing its production while reducing costs, which will improve FCF.

The company's ability to drive FCF after these investments is key to future returns.

Petrobras Shareholder Returns

The company's overall debt has hit a very manageable level, which enables it to focus on shareholder returns.

The company's average debt maturity has remained manageable at 12.2 years, despite the high interest rate environment that we're in. At the same time, the company has continued to improve its risk profile with its spread to the US treasury rate now sitting at a mere 2.6%. That is in a higher interest rate environment, but it's still a strong improvement.

The company's financial obligations are primarily leasing with the company's net debt at $56 billion and financial debt at a mere net of $15.3 billion. That's a very manageable level to the company's market cap.

The company remains committed to its strong dividends. The company's announced dividends of $0.16 are $0.32 / ADR share or a ~3% yield. The company plans to pay these dividends in late August and September showing its continued commitment to shareholder returns based on its 45% FCF remuneration policy.

As long as the company's share price remains low it's sitting at a double-digit dividend yield, highlighting its financial strength.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is crude oil prices. The company is fundamentally an oil producer and seller with a focus on exporting that oil. The company as a result is dependent on Brent prices for cash flow, prices that are currently weak as tariffs and markets make demand uncertain. That lack of cash flow fundamentally hurts the company's ability to drive FCF.

The second piece of this is the uncertainty with the Brazilian government. The company has faced massive corruption scandals with the government and in many ways, its revenue is used as a way to benefit Brazil versus the company's shareholder returns. Continued government risk could hurt the company's ability to drive returns.

Conclusion

Petrobras has an impressive portfolio of assets. The company is actively revamping its portfolio and is bringing online new FPSOs supporting increasing production as it invests massively within its business. That has led to substantial lease debt for the company, but it's debts that the company can comfortably afford.

The company does have risks as a result of oil prices along with governance risks with the Brazilian government's control, however, overall, we expect the company to be able to continue driving substantial shareholder returns. That makes the company a valuable investment opportunity. Please let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

data.title: Buying PepsiCo With A Rising Dividend At A Discount
data.description:
Introduction

My investing guidelines and portfolio focus are on investing for income that provides ample yield, safety, and annual increases that are above my expected long-term rate of inflation (3%). I want rising dividends because I want rising income during my retirement. I also require the stocks I invest in to have demonstrated a competitive advantage that has proven unassailable for many years. That proof comes in the form of stable and predictable financial results even through periods of market turmoil like we had in 2001-03 and 2007-09. PepsiCo (NASDAQ:PEP) always survives and is able to thrive in the aftermath.

In addition to those requirements, I look for stocks of companies that are trading at what I have determined as a discount to fair value. I want a compound annual return of at least 10%. I do not plan on selling for at least five years. I have been retired since 2002 and continue to build additional layers of income each year through dividends. Layer upon layer of income that increases every year is my formula for a worry-free retirement.

I want to begin by pointing out that the equity universe is divided today into two groups: The New World Economy (Mega Cap Growth) and the Old World Economy.

New World Economy

The New World Economy is made up primarily of mega cap technology companies, along with some peripheral businesses that benefit the most from enabling the New World Economy, such as large financial institutions and suppliers.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average Index (DIA), NASDAQ 100 (QQQ), and the S&P 500 Index (SPY) are dominated by these behemoths due to weighting, especially QQQ and SPY. Both indexes are weighted by market capitalization, which means that the largest companies can dominate the index movements. The largest companies, such as Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), Nvidia (NVDA), Alphabet (GOOGL), Meta (META), and Tesla (TSLA), range in market cap from $1 trillion to $3 trillion. Smaller companies that are not involved in AI (Artificial Intelligence) or EVs (Electric Vehicles) cannot influence the index with relatively small capitalizations of $10 billion to $300 billion.

Old World Economy

Those small companies make up the vast majority of the economy and that is why I call them the Old World Economy. Companies like PepsiCo, with a market capitalization of a mere $180 billion, are in the S&P 500 but have little influence over how the index moves.

When passive investors buy shares of SPY or QQQ, most of those dollars end up in the mega cap companies’ shares because of the weighting. When investors buy shares in one or more of the mega cap companies, the entire index is lifted. Since the advent of AI investing, very little money is flowing into shares of those companies that represent the majority of the economy.

It is like the shares of much of our economy got left behind even though the “market” is flirting with making new highs.

Why PepsiCo shares have fallen

There is a lot of chatter about how the weight-loss drugs, GLP-1 drugs like Ozempic and Wegovy (among others), are reducing demand for sodas and snacks, the core of PepsiCo’s business. But I have another take on why demand has stagnated.

As pointed out in a very well-written analysis by Eugenio Catone, aggressive price hikes have forced consumers to think twice about buying that bag of potato chips. Price increases of 14% in 2022 and 13% in 2023 of some of its popular product lines caused a decrease in unit demand.

But there is more to the price drop than stagnant demand. Also, from the same article linked above, the cost of capital is weighing on PepsiCo because the company has taken on debt to facilitate acquisitions in recent years at a rate that is much higher than is customary. This is temporary for two reasons: First, borrowing costs will eventually come down over the next two to three years as the Fed cut interest rates. Second, PepsiCo will adjust its capital allocation over the next few years to include less funding committed to acquisitions.

The perception of the investing public is driving the price down with worry about the company’s ability to maintain its dividend. This will become a non-issue due to steps I expect the company to take as described in the previous paragraph which will allow the company to generate more free cash flow. That, in turn, will enable PepsiCo to continue it streak of raising its dividend annually for 52 consecutive years. Over the last five years, the company has raised its dividend by an average of 7.25% per year. I expect that rate of increase will slow to 6% per year going forward as the company finds it somewhat more difficult to achieve the levels of earnings growth necessary to support the higher rate of increase.

I believe that there is concern over the long-term impact of GLP-1 drugs reducing the craving for snacks and sodas. I’m not buying it! Many (not all) of the people who are using weight loss drugs will go right back to their old eating habits as soon as they go off the meds. They are losing weight so they can enjoy the life they want to live and that life will include snacks and sodas.

The world population continues to grow and PepsiCo, while already operating in 200 countries has more penetration to accomplish in many of the emerging markets around the world before it hits saturation. Unit growth will return and profits will climb.

Calculating the Expected Total Return

Mr. Catone estimated that the shares are 15-20% undervalued. The shares have fallen by almost 24% over the last year and by 33.8% from the all-time high achieved in May 2023.

Looking back at history, PepsiCo’s average P/E (price-to-earnings) ratio over the last ten years is about 22.4, while it is currently trading at 16.5 times earnings. This means that as growth in revenue and profits return to normal levels, so should the multiple, which equates to a discount of about 35%. This assumes that interest rates fall to more favorable levels to reduce the company’s cost of capital and interest expense in the future. I do not expect the shares to rebound overnight, but do believe there will be a reversion to the mean effect over the next five years.

If we assume the multiple expansion is achieved by May 2030, we can assume that PepsiCo’s share price will rise by an average of 6.3% per year to accommodate a reversion to the mean in valuation.

(((22.4/16.5)^(1/5))-1)= 6.3%

Add to that the current dividend yield of 4.3% along with an expected growth in earnings of 6% per year and we end up with an expected total annual return of 16.7% over the next five years.

Risks

The most obvious risk is that I could be wrong about consumer habits changing due to GLP-1 drugs. If that is the case, and people who lose weight using these drugs stay away from (or dramatically reduce intake of) sodas and salty snacks permanently, there is a risk that growth may never recover.

Another potential risk is that management does not slow down on acquisitions and continues to pile up debt to unsustainable levels. This would reduce the company's long-term capital allocation flexibility and increase interest expense. The result would be that the company could no longer continue to increase the dividend annually as it has done for 52 years.

While this last risk is possible, I do not think that management will risk breaking the company's record of rising dividends. Although such things have happened in other companies, and it could happen to PepsiCo, my sincere belief is that management will do whatever it takes to maintain that streak.

That brings us to a third potential risk. If the company continues to use debt to acquire brands with future growth, it may also need to use debt to fund the dividends. Such a model is not sustainable over the long term and could prevent the company from maintaining its stellar credit rating, thereby increasing its cost of capital. Again, I believe management at PepsiCo is cognizant of these risks and will avoid making such mistakes. However, it behooves us, as investors, to consider all the possibilities.

Conclusion

Many companies like PepsiCo have been left behind in the Old World Economy valuation scheme that is predominant today. I believe that paradigm will be reevaluated over the next 3-5 years to the benefit of steady, predictable growth and rising dividends. The volatility of the New World Economy stocks will eventually unnerve enough investors and drive them back to the basics of fundamental investing. That will favor dividend champions like PepsiCo.

I believe the dividend is safe and will continue to grow at about the rate of future earnings growth (6% per year). Locking in a potential 16.7% compound annual return on an investment today is a good choice. I rate PepsiCo stock a buy.

data.title: Cadence Bank: Shares Get Cheaper Amid Mounting Economic Uncertainty
data.description:
It has been around eight months since I first covered Southern lender Cadence Bank (NYSE:CADE). Looking back, my bullish case for Cadence really rested on its lending margin potential, as this bank simply stood to gain more from balance sheet repricing dynamics than peers did. That has largely played, albeit to no effect on the stock, with these shares actually performing a little worse than the broader regional bank space in that time.

While that period has seen plenty of economic turbulence - with the market still weighing up the impact of the current administration's trade policies - I'd note that consensus earnings estimates aren't actually any lower for Cadence than they were back then. Given that maps to some relatively cheap-looking valuation multiples, I remain bullish.

NIM Expansion Plays Out, But Pre-Provision Growth Has Been Weak

After lagging when rates were rising, I thought lower interest rates would help Cadence more than the industry. Put simply, while Cadence's funding costs had risen sharply, it had simultaneously seen a relatively modest pass-through rate on its assets during the hiking cycle, squeezing its net interest margin.

As expected, NIM trends have been good since my opening piece. On the liability side, the Fed's rate cuts have driven around 40bps of improvement in Cadence's interest costs (expressed relative to total interest-earning assets). On the asset side, Cadence's yield has fallen by a much more modest 19bps, aided by fixed-rate assets repricing upwards. This has resulted in roughly 20bps of total NIM expansion, which is a better performance than the banking system as a whole (up around 9bps in that time to 3.25%).

Unfortunately, balance sheet growth appears to have been a weak spot for Cadence. Interest-earning assets have fallen around 3% since my opening piece to $43.6 billion, with Cadence underperforming the wider industry here. As a result, margin improvement has only led to relatively modest growth in quarterly net interest revenue, which has risen by less than 2% to around $363 million.

This has driven a disappointing performance on the pre-provision line. Whereas the industry has seen roughly mid-single-digit growth in pretax pre-provision profit in that time, Cadence's has been virtually flat after adjusting for one-off items.

Positively, management estimates that both deposits and loans will grow low-to-mid-single-digits this year, and that revenue growth will outpace cost growth, implying a return to pre-provision profit growth.

Shares Flat As Attention Turns To Tariffs

Per the introduction, Cadence's stock has ended up about flat since my last piece, albeit with large movements on either side. For all the recent turbulence, I would note Cadence's bottom line outlook remains largely unchanged. Consensus 2025 EPS ($2.89) is around 3% higher than it was back then, and while 2026 consensus has seen a larger jump (to $3.41), this is partly due to deal accretion relating to Cadence's upcoming acquisition of Industry Bankshares. Excluding this, standalone 2026 EPS projections also haven't moved much (consensus was around $3.10-$3.15 per share before the deal announcement).

When I opened on it, Cadence was trading for around 12x then-forward EPS. Given the current $30.23 quote, and assuming 2025 plays out as the sell-side expects it to, Cadence's current forward P/E is only around 10.5x. That falls to less than 9x based on the 2026 consensus. Similarly, Cadence has delivered 11% growth in tangible book value per share since my opening piece (to $22.30). Since the share price has been flat, its P/TBVPS multiple has contracted by around 15bps to 1.35x.

Looking back, both of those metrics are now at double-digit discounts to their recent historical averages. Cadence's trailing 12-month dividend yield is also around 30% above its ten-year average, as shown below.

Granted, attention has increasingly turned to the possible negative impact of U.S. trade policy on the economy, but I don't think credit risk represents an outsized issue for Cadence. Cadence's net charge-offs have been flat in recent quarters. At 27bps, they are also roughly in line with the peer average. Ditto for its non-performing loans ratio, which has now registered two sequential periods of improvement.

It is true that Cadence does more lending than the average bank, as loans constitute just over 70% of its assets compared to a circa 65% average for peers. In a downturn, names with less loan concentration may be a safer bet.

More important than this is that, one, Cadence has an above-average NIM, and two, at over 1.6% in 1Q25, it also earns an above-average pre-provision return on its assets. Put simply, while Cadence does more lending than the average bank, it also has a greater ability to absorb credit charges.

Cadence's capital cushion also appears to be healthy. There will be some dilution from the Industry deal due to fair value accounting, but a projected post-close CET1 ratio of 11.0% remains solid.

Looking back, Cadence has grown earnings per share by 115% over the past decade (equal to roughly 8% annualized). Adjusted for the unrealized losses on its bond portfolio, tangible book value per share has risen by around 80% in that time (equal to just over 6% annualized).

Cadence's current quarterly dividend is $0.275 per share, for a conservative payout ratio of roughly 40% of net income. Cadence has plenty of room to fund strong earnings and balance sheet growth ahead, including inorganically via share buybacks and more M&A.

A sub-9x P/E on the 2026 consensus looks much too cheap for this. If Cadence simply traded on its current 10.5x forward EPS multiple a year from now, the stock could offer ~18%-19% upside, rising to ~22% including the dividend.

Conclusion

Cadence Bank has been a somewhat disappointing performer since I first covered it last September. Net interest margin expansion has largely played out as I had hoped but, after a lot of volatility, the shares have remained virtually flat. Taking the positive from that, this performance means that Cadence has become even cheaper than it was back then, with its P/E ratio, P/TBVPS multiple, and dividend yield all looking attractive based on their past averages. While tariff turmoil could hit banks in the near term, I don't have any particular concerns with Cadence from a credit perspective. Now looks like a good entry point, and I remain bullish.

data.title: Rebuilt And Re-Rated: Woodward Belongs In The Quality Growth Bucket
data.description:
Woodward (NASDAQ:WWD) is no longer just a cyclical industrial parts supplier—it's evolving into a precision systems platform company with strong exposure to structurally growing markets like defense, aerospace aftermarket, and energy resilience. This strategic shift is starting to be recognized by the market, as reflected in the recent breakout of the stock. Investors are increasingly buying into the idea that Woodward isn’t just rebounding—it’s building a sustained, high-margin growth trajectory.

At the core of the investment thesis is Woodward’s aerospace segment, which now accounts for over 60% of total revenue and remains its most profitable business line. Within aerospace, defense OEM is showing exceptional momentum—up 52% in Q2—driven by demand from long-cycle Smart Defense programs like DDG-51, JDAM, and KDDX. These programs come with deep backlogs and contracts that extend beyond 2025, offering visibility and consistency.

Backing all of this is a structurally improving margin profile. Aerospace margins hit a record 22.2% in Q2, thanks to scale, lean manufacturing, and smarter pricing. The company is now guiding to normalized incremental margins of 30–35% in the second half—solid numbers that suggest profitability is sustainable, not propped up by short-term gains.

For investors, the story here is compelling. Woodward is transitioning into a margin-accretive growth name—a rare setup in the mid-cap industrial space. With demand tailwinds in place, pricing power holding, and execution solid, there’s still meaningful upside as volumes normalize and the market continues to re-rate the stock accordingly. Let’s take a deeper look and break it down.

Top-Line Growth Drivers

Aerospace is clearly picking up speed again, and it’s happening across all segments. In the commercial aftermarket, Q2 saw a 23% jump year-over-year, thanks to strong demand for maintenance and high aircraft utilization. That momentum isn’t expected to fade anytime soon—management’s still guiding for high single-digit growth in the second half, which puts Woodward ahead of most players in the space.

On the defense side, OEM sales surged 52% from a year ago, powered by demand from Smart Defense initiatives and strength in propulsion systems tied to key platforms like the DDG-51 and KDDX. These programs run long and are riding geopolitical tailwinds, giving the segment strong visibility. Meanwhile, the commercial OE side has hit a temporary speed bump due to ongoing issues at Boeing. Still, with 787 production ramping back up—particularly around GE’s GEnx engines—there’s good reason to believe activity will pick up again soon, which bodes well for Woodward’s exposure through GE.

Zooming out, aerospace now makes up around 64% of total revenue and stands as Woodward’s most profitable and durable growth engine. It gives the company a solid foothold in long-term themes like the post-pandemic recovery in air travel, rising global defense budgets, and increasing production of next-gen platforms.

As for the industrial side of the business, it’s holding up well in spite of a 5% overall decline. When you exclude China, core industrial sales were actually up 11% year-over-year. Oil and gas led the way with 21% growth, tied to infrastructure expansion, LNG projects, and broader power generation demand. Marine transportation climbed 13%, benefiting from a shipbuilding boom expected to last into the next decade. Power generation posted a modest 4% gain, lifted by growing needs from data centers and grid resilience investments. This side of the business helps cushion the ups and downs of aerospace and keeps earnings steadier. With exposure to China now limited and under control, the rest of the industrial portfolio is showing healthy global demand tied to energy and infrastructure trends.

Margin Expansion Drivers

Margins, especially in aerospace, are in a good place. Segment margin in Q2 hit 22.2%, a company record and up 240 basis points from last year. That kind of gain didn’t come from just one lever—it was a mix of stronger pricing, a favorable aftermarket skew, and disciplined cost management through lean manufacturing. For the second half, Woodward’s expecting incrementals of 30–35%. That’s below the 40% hit in Q2 but still solid, and a sign that margins are moving in the right direction for the long haul. Even with more volume coming from lower-margin OE and defense work, the structural economics still look strong.

Pricing is also playing a bigger role. In Q2, it contributed around 7% to total growth, with aerospace again leading the charge. Woodward’s getting better at charging what its platforms are worth—especially in mission-critical areas where performance and reliability matter most. The company’s value-based pricing toolkit seems to be working, and investors tend to reward firms that can turn technical strength into margin durability.

Operationally, things continue to improve. Facilities like Rockford and Zealand are seeing the benefits of production upgrades, smoother onboarding, and greater throughput—all while keeping costs down. The regional supply chain strategy also helps by reducing the impact of tariffs and currency swings. These gains aren’t just cyclical boosts—they’re baked into how the company runs, and the margin benefits should keep building over time.

By the Numbers

Q2 FY25 came in strong, with revenue at $884 million—up 6% from the year before. Aerospace was up 13%, and core industrial climbed 11%. Adjusted earnings per share landed at $1.69, or $1.78 on a GAAP basis, compared to $1.62 last year. Free cash flow hit $60 million for the first half, a solid number considering the working capital investment. Aerospace margins reached an all-time high of 22.2%, and the company bumped up the low end of its full-year EPS guidance to $5.95–$6.25, reflecting confidence in how the rest of the year will play out.

What’s Getting Investors Excited

There are a few long-term themes that are helping boost investor confidence. The continued ramp in LEAP and GTF aftermarket demand brings a steady, multi-decade growth tailwind from narrowbody aircraft. Defense OEM remains a strong, counter-cyclical growth driver with solid visibility.

Marine and power generation might be flying under the radar, but they’re proving to be solid contributors thanks to global infrastructure demand. Pricing power is helping protect margins and improve returns on capital. With China now isolated within the portfolio, Woodward has optionality if it ever wants to divest. And with $215 million in planned capital returns for FY25, supported by strong free cash flow, the company has plenty of room to reward shareholders.

Risk Snapshot

Even with all the momentum Woodward has built, there are a few areas that could trip things up in the short term. First, aerospace is still cyclical at its core—any slowdown in OEM production or a dip in aftermarket activity could soften both revenue and margins. Geopolitics could be another wildcard. If demand from China stalls or tariffs flare up again, that’s a direct hit to top-line growth and potentially input costs. Then there’s product mix: if more volume shifts toward OE and less toward aftermarket, margins could take a hit, since OE typically isn’t as profitable. Execution matters too—any supply chain hiccups or delays in key program ramps could create choppiness in both margins and cash flow. And finally, with expectations now running high, there’s always the risk of valuation pressure if the company underdelivers.

None of these are deal-breakers for the long-term story. Woodward is still evolving into a more focused, higher-margin systems business. But these risks could throw off the near-term trajectory. For the next couple of quarters, it’s worth keeping an eye on how quickly the 787 and LEAP engine ramps materialize, whether aftermarket demand—especially out of China—starts to cool, and how well the company keeps margins intact as the product mix shifts. Also watch delivery performance on key defense programs like JDAM and DDG-51. If those stay on track, the bigger picture remains intact.

Wrapping It Up: What This Means for Woodward’s Valuation Path

If you’re trying to get a sense of who Woodward is really competing with, it helps to look beyond just traditional industrial names. On the aerospace side, TransDigm (TDG) is probably the closest match. They’re both in the business of making high-stakes, mission-critical components—stuff that’s built to last and tough to replace. TransDigm’s focus on the aftermarket and strong margins lines up well with Woodward’s model, especially as Woodward leans harder into aerospace systems and platforms.

On the industrial front, Regal Rexnord (RRX) is a good comp—not because it’s identical, but because it operates in overlapping areas like motion control, energy systems, and electrification. It’s a more diversified business, but when you’re looking at how Woodward’s industrial segment performs or is valued, Regal is a decent reality check.

Then there’s the growing defense angle. Woodward’s involvement in programs like JDAM and DDG-51 puts it in the same conversation as defense-focused players like L3Harris (LHX). L3’s core is defense electronics and C4ISR systems—high-tech, highly reliable gear that operates in serious environments. That overlap in engineering complexity and long-cycle contracts makes L3Harris a useful reference when thinking about where Woodward’s defense business could go.

At a high level, Woodward looks like a company in the middle of a re-rating. The stock has been climbing alongside some of the best operators in the aerospace space—names like TransDigm—and that’s not a coincidence. The market is starting to price in the fact that Woodward’s not just another industrial name anymore.

Now, to be clear, it’s not trading at a bargain. With a price-to-earnings multiple around 37.9x and price-to-sales at 4.3x, the stock is already getting some premium treatment. But it's still sitting below TransDigm on those same metrics. If Woodward can keep pushing margins higher and hold its current mix—especially with more aftermarket contribution—there’s room for that multiple to stretch even further.

What’s also notable is how it stacks up against peers. It now trades at a premium to more diversified industrials like Regal Rexnord and even to big defense names like L3Harris. That premium suggests the market is buying into the hybrid model—part aerospace systems, part industrial controls—and the stability that comes with it.

The real upside from here likely comes down to a few key things: continued margin improvement, steady strength in the aftermarket, and reliable execution on major programs like JDAM, LEAP, and DDG-51. If those boxes get checked, there’s a solid case for further multiple expansion. At this juntucture, I see good signs supporting the main thesis, therefore, I am rating it a buy.

data.title: QINT: Good Companies, Bad Timing; Hold
data.description:
Introduction

The American Century Quality Diversified International ETF (NYSEARCA:QINT) is a very diversified fund that holds names across non-US stocks. It is a good way to gain exposure to international companies while still having a spread-out, diverse approach within your portfolio. Here are some of the key facts about the fund below, taken from the prospectus.

  • Expense Ratio: 0.39%
  • AUM: $339M
  • YTD Return: 21.3%

Their top 10 holdings comprise 13% of their portfolio, and are shown below:

The countries that are represented are also highlighted below:

We like QINT’s approach to diversification across industries while refraining from US exposure. In the following section, we outline 3 names that QINT did well to include.

Top Holdings Analysis

Novartis: This Swiss drugmaker is the largest holding of QINT, and rightfully so—it is one of the major global pharmaceutical players, and they are constantly innovating. A report this week from Novartis revealed that Pluvicto, a targeted radiotherapy drug, has been shown to actively slow the progression of certain types of prostate cancer, which is of course fantastic news for more reasons than just the stock market. These are still very early-stage tests, and there is much more to go for something like this to 1) pass regulators and 2) materially add to their bottom line, but it is clear that despite being a whale in the world of biopharma, Novartis continues to grow and innovate. They are planning to request approval from regulators in the latter half of the year. Another point of observation is that Novartis is willing to leverage AI to better refine its drug development approach. A partnership was recently announced with Viz AI to provide care for cancer patients, which is great not only for the brand but allows Novartis to apply the most cutting-edge drugs to use under this AI-powered care management program. This partnership will focus on identifying crucial risk factors related to breast and prostate cancers, and they will be able to outline precise and coordinated care across the physician spectrum and team to best provide for the patient. Novartis is a great addition to this ETF because they consistently innovate, aim to partner with the leading technology players, and are up ~19% YTD.

Hermes: This famous French luxury goods maker recently overtook LVMH as the most valuable luxury company in the world—a tough feat considering many other luxury brands are not doing as well in this turbulent market. Hermes is all about restricting supply, which promotes an air of rareness and desirability within their consumer (and want-to-be consumer) bases—both of which are key facets of a successful luxury brand. They are able to stick to the upper end of the luxury spectrum and refrain from sales, promotions, discounts, or anything of that nature to minimize the reputational impacts on their brand. Furthermore, the company sticks to its tried and true strategy of increasing prices (on a CAGR basis since launch, their flagship bag, the Birkin, has increased 4-6% annually). This regularly recurring hike in prices helps to shield the company from seasonal impacts, things like tariffs, and other events that may cause margins to slip. Despite all of this — the limited supply, and the price increases — consumers are still more in love with Hermes than ever. In fact, to meet the unstoppable demand, the company has said that 4 new factories will be opened in France by 2028 to meet this insatiable desire for Hermes goods. These factories will hire hundreds of top-class artisans to make its famous bags and leather equestrian goods, and should only help the brand in acquiring even more consumers and ship more products. Hermes is a valuable addition to the ETF for these reasons.

Sony: Sony has a major boost to its revenues coming up in the next ~2 years that should help boost its stock price significantly—the impending release of the next-generation console known as the PlayStation 6. Reports are showing that the earliest release date would be sometime in early 2027, which would complete the average ~7-year cycle that PS consoles typically have in the market. Considering that Sony consoles have the largest market share, this is great news for the firm.

Sony as a firm is also becoming more and more streamlined. In the latest announcement of its multi-year transformation into the Sony of the future, the company has released a plan that proposes the spin-off of its finance arm. This would make Sony more streamlined and hopefully, evaluated in the market less as a conglomerate but more as a pure-technology firm. This should increase its multiples and valuation prospects down the line if this breakout of financials does occur. We are bullish on Sony as a company and also believe that the mid-term catalyst of the PS6 launch should help to garner interest in the company and stock in the meantime.

Historical Valuation

In order to understand the relative valuation of QINT, we look at the P/E ratio of its top holdings compared to recent history.

The above charts show the P/E ratio of QINT’s top 3 holdings (Novartis, Roche, and HSBC) over the last 3 years. The P/E ratio growth for each over that time period is:

Company

3-Year P/E Growth

Novartis

+119.25%

Roche

+55.21%

HSBC

-6.01%

Novartis and Roche have both shown a significant increase in P/E over the last 3 years, while HSBC has actually shown a lower P/E. On the surface level, it looks like Novartis and Roche may be overvalued. In order to better understand their P/E growth, we look at their earnings (the “E” in P/E).

As you can see, during the 3-year period, the earnings for both Novartis and Roche have dropped by a non-trivial amount. For both companies, we see that the decreasing earnings is the biggest driving factor in P/E change, especially for Novartis. We see this as a big red flag. While a higher P/E is never a good indication for valuations, it is especially a bad sign when the P/E growth is driven by decreasing earnings as opposed to increasing prices.

On the flip side, HSBC has actually shown earnings growth, and it is likely a big driver for its decrease in P/E.

We find it concerning that Novartis and Roche have had such big P/E growth driven by declining earnings. However, against the same metric, HSBC looks to be relatively undervalued compared to its recent history.

Of course, it is important to note that the top 3 holdings make up only 4.51% of the total fund. Because of this, we did a similar analysis of the rest of the top 10 holdings as well. In order to avoid spamming 14 more screenshots in this article, we will instead summarize our findings. When we look further down the list of top holdings, we see a mixed bag. Some names like Engie and Hermes had a similar pattern as Novartis/Roche with increasing P/E and declining or flat earnings, while other names like BNP showed increasing earnings and declining P/E.

Overall, based on a P/E analysis, we consider QINT as overvalued. Yes, they have some top names with attractive P/E compared to recent history, but they also have several top names that have significantly increasing P/E coupled with declining earnings. This fact, paired with their YTD performance, leads us to believe that it may be time for a ‘cooling-off’ period for international names—especially as US markets become safer and more attractive for investment again.

Risks

Tariffs

As an international-focused ETF, QINT has major potential risks when it comes to tariffs with the recent US escalations. Since the United States has the largest consumer market in the world, increasing tariffs and/or reducing access to that market could materially impact the performance of QINT's holdings.

Diversification

QINT is a relatively diversified ETF, with the top 10 names making up only 12.99% of the overall holdings. One risk to our overvalued stance is that we looked mainly at the top holdings. Since QINT is not very highly concentrated, there could be more undervalued companies in the rest of the fund’s holdings that can make a non-trivial impact on the fund’s valuation profile.

Conclusion

We are quite bullish on the top names in QINT. Novartis remains a large yet innovative drugmaker, with ongoing trials for game-changing cancer treatments. Hermes is the largest luxury brand in the world, having surpassed LVMH in size, and shows no signs of slowing down—in fact, more factories are being opened in the next 3 years. Sony remains a market leader in consumer technologies and should have a strong catalyst upcoming over the next few years with the release of the PS6 console. These are all strong names to have in the fund. However, despite the overall favorable position of each firm, we cannot ignore the valuation. A company could have high-growth prospects and macro tailwinds, but at the end of the day, valuation is the key factor. We would rather invest in a low-quality company that is 50% undervalued than a high-quality company that is 50% overvalued.

data.title: Yellow Cake: Still Trades At A Substantial Discount To The Long-Term Uranium Price
data.description:
Overview

Yellow Cake (OTCQX:YLLXF) is a passive investment company that owns uranium. The stock is listed in the UK and there is also an OTC listing in the United States. Yellow Cake is relatively similar to the Sprott Physical Uranium Trust (OTCPK:SRUUF). I have covered Yellow Cake regularly going back to late 2019 and my prior articles on the company can be found here.

The stock price performance of Yellow Cake has increased 161% since the beginning of this decade. That is slightly below what the spot price of uranium has done during this period and marginally better than how the long-term uranium price has performed.

The minor annual operating cost, which is now below 1% of the net asset value ("NAV"), is one reason why Yellow Cake has underperformed the spot price of uranium slightly. The other reason is because the discount to net asset value is today marginally larger than what it was at the end of 2019.

Recent Developments

The spot price of uranium and most uranium equities made a high in the beginning of 2024 and has since then corrected substantially. The spot price of uranium did at the April-low have a 40% drawdown from the recent peak, Yellow Cake almost declined 50% from its recent peak while junior uranium equities (URNJ) experienced a 60% drawdown.

Uranium equities are cyclical and over the years have shown to be very sensitive to overall stock market weakness. The levels seen in April were rather extreme, especially when we consider that the long-term contract price has barely declined anything here.

The sentiment has been poor for uranium equities for about a year, which was exacerbated by the overall stock market turbulence in April. This period resembles what we saw in 2020, when uranium equities had been weak for a while leading up to the Q1 2020 sell-off. Many uranium equities rebounded during the following months, but the real takeoff for uranium equities happened in late 2020 and 2021.

We have over the last couple of months seen a noticeable recovery, where some of the most oversold uranium equities have rebounded as much as 50% from the lows. However, most uranium equities and Yellow Cake continue to trade at very attractive levels.

The nuclear industry continues to see positive news being announced, where a few recent international developments are that Denmark has lifted its 40-year ban on nuclear power and Belgium has reversed its nuclear phase-out policy. Germany has also signaled that it no longer objects to nuclear power to achieve CO2 emissions targets within the European Union. Following this string of news, the list of European Union countries that now oppose nuclear power is starting to become very short.

In May, President Trump also signed executive orders with the aim of quadrupling nuclear power by 2050. That is probably more of an aspirational goal, which will be lowered over time. Regardless, concrete steps are taken in both Europe and North America building out nuclear power again. Several Asian countries and China in particular are already today aggressively building new nuclear reactors. So, it is hard not to be very bullish on nuclear energy and consequently uranium in the long run.

Earlier this week, we also had Meta (META) sign a 20-year power purchase agreement from Constellation Energy (CEG), for nuclear power. So, the demand from datacenters and the large U.S. tech companies is having an impact in the here and now as well.

Valuation & Conclusion

Using the latest £4.92 stock price of Yellow Cake, the current GBPUSD rate of 1.36, and a uranium spot price of $70.55/lb. Yellow Cake is today trading with a 6.2% discount to net asset value, which equates to an implied uranium price of $66.1/lb.

While the discount to spot has decreased some lately for Yellow Cake, the stock still trades with a very attractive implied uranium price, where a large percentage of uranium producers have all-in costs well above that level.

In the chart below, we can see the comparison of Yellow Cake and the long-term contract price for uranium over the years. The discount has come down from the 30+% that we saw in 2020 and March-April this year, but the discount for Yellow Cake is still at a healthy 17% to the long-term contract price of uranium.

I am long Yellow Cake, and I rate the stock a buy, as I see the uranium spot and contract markets strengthening as the contracting volume picks up going forward. The rating is down from a strong buy at the time of my last article in March.

We have for over a decade been below replacement level of contracting, which is likely to be the main catalyst going forward along with all the positive industry developments for nuclear energy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Diamond Hill Select Strategy Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Commentary

Markets were mixed in Q1 as the new presidential administration made repeated waves with an avalanche of activity, which left markets uncertain of the likely path forward. For the quarter, US stocks fell -4.7% (as measured by the Russell 3000 Index), notching markets’ first quarterly decline since Q3 2023. Mid-cap stocks held up best, falling a more modest -3.4%, while large caps were down -4.5%. Smaller stocks were hit harder, falling -9.5% — a relatively unsurprising development given smaller companies’ shares are generally more volatile as macroeconomic uncertainty increases. From a style perspective, value fared better up and down the cap spectrum, with large-cap value managing a positive gain of +2.1%. Mid-cap value stocks fell -2.1%, compared to -7.1% for their growth counterparts, while small-cap value stocks declined -7.7% versus -11.1% for small growth (all returns as measured by their respective Russell indices). The shift from growth to value was also relatively unsurprising given the flight to safety prompted by tremendous uncertainty around trade and other fiscal policy shifts in the US.

From a sector perspective, likewise, more defensive sectors in the Russell 3000 Index led in the quarter, while cyclical stocks fell out of favor. Energy stocks were the best performers, up +8.0% amid supportive language from President Trump’s administration about its willingness to increase US oil production. Utilities also rose nicely, up +5.2%, as did consumer staples, which rose +4.8% and health care, which gained +4.6%. Conversely, consumer discretionary stocks were hit hardest, declining -13.3%, while technology stocks also fell -13.0% — which, given the sector’s weight in the index, was a major contributor to the index’s negative Q1 return.

What began in Q4 — market leadership transitions in anticipation of potential winners and losers from a major shift in executive-level policy direction — vigorously continued in Q1. To some extent, economic data took a backseat in the quarter as investors seemed to focus narrowly on the likeliest implications of major trade policy shifts announced during the quarter by President Trump. Shortly after the quarter concluded, Trump announced a raft of tariffs to go into effect immediately on what he dubbed “Liberation Day.” As has been the case since Trump began increasing tariffs, countries are responding in various ways, with some reaching out to the US to negotiate better rates and others — notably, China — choosing to retaliate with their own high tariff rates. Many companies have preemptively announced significant investments in their US operations — ostensibly moves aimed at avoiding tariffs themselves. Ultimately, whether the “Liberation Day” moniker goes down in history or infamy remains to be seen. What is clear is markets are very nervous about the president’s apparent determination to wholesale (and possibly singlehandedly) rework the global trade landscape, even with countries that have historically been significant trade allies, like Taiwan. We’ll have more thoughts on potential direction from here later in this communication.

Meanwhile, the Federal Reserve held rates in March, citing the likelihood tariffs increase inflationary pressures, while cutting its growth expectations for US GDP in 2025. And the US dollar — which one would generally expect to be bolstered relative to other global currencies as tariffs go up (higher prices for US goods increase demand for US dollars with which to pay for them) — counterintuitively fell as tariff language toughened, perhaps signaling investors’ growing concerns tariffs will contribute to a stagflationary US economic environment with high inflation and low or sluggish growth.

Globally, geopolitical uncertainty remains high, though there have been some signs of modest progress toward ending the war between Russia and Ukraine. Following a dramatic fall-out between President Trump and Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy, the US and Ukraine seemed to slowly make their way back toward a minerals and mining deal which would meaningfully increase the US’s economic involvement in Ukraine and potentially pave the way for a broader peace deal between Russia and Ukraine. However, to date, Russian President Vladimir Putin has stymied the US’s attempts to broker a ceasefire between the two countries. War in the Middle East continues, too, as ceasefires between Israel and Hamas and Israel and Lebanon ended and violence across the region re- escalated. The US, too, struck Houthi rebels in Yemen toward the end of the quarter in an attempt to quell attacks on Red Sea shipping lanes.

While volatility is unsettling, it also introduces interesting investing opportunities for those who have been diligent in their research all along. We have no unique insight into how deep this correction will become, whether it will enter bear market territory or how long it will last. However, we know it has helped reset valuations and offered some compelling opportunities to purchase high-quality names at more attractive prices than we’ve seen in some time. Once the trade-related dust settles, time will tell which sectors and individual companies are winners and losers. In the meantime, we will continue deploying our time-tested approach, which is well-built for uncertain market environments like we’re currently seeing — and which we wouldn’t be surprised to see persist for the foreseeable future.

Performance Discussion

Our portfolio held up better than the Russell 3000 Index in Q1. Relative strength was concentrated in our financials exposure — both our overweight to the outperforming sector as well as our individual holdings, which outpaced benchmark peers. Our below-benchmark exposure to the technology sector was also a relative tailwind as the sector materially underperformed in Q1. Conversely, relative weakness was concentrated mainly in our industrials holdings.

Among our top individual contributors in Q1 were Mr. Cooper Group (COOP) and Huntington Ingalls (HII). Mortgage servicing company Mr. Cooper Group executed well during a particularly challenging mortgage environment as interest rates have remained high relative to historical levels.

Importantly, Mr. Cooper’s business model is specifically designed to help protect it from just such periods: As rates go up, the value of its mortgage-servicing rights business goes up, giving it the ability to continue generating healthy cash flows even as the mortgage industry overall may slow.

Shares of US Department of Defense and Navy supplier Huntington Ingalls rose in Q1 following President Trump’s announced formation of a new office of shipbuilding. The president further made comments suggesting the government is working to help Huntington Ingalls address recent supply chain and labor issues, which we anticipate will be resolved over time. We maintain our conviction in Huntington Ingalls’ positioning relative to the Department of Defense’s need to prepare for a potential conflict in the Pacific — which would in turn ensure strong demand for the next 5 to 10 years. Further, we believe resolving the aforementioned supply chain and labor issues will allow Huntington Ingalls to improve its margins — a likelihood that we believe is not yet reflected in the share price.

Other top contributors in Q1 included American International Group (AIG), Abbott Laboratories (ABT) and SS&C Technologies (SSNC). Property and casualty insurance company AIG is delivering high-quality results: Its most recently reported underlying loss ratio was better than expected, as were catastrophe losses and prior-year development.

Further, the company continues generating revenue growth from both its commercial and consumer segments. We are pleased with the progress AIG is making to streamline its business and capitalize on opportunities for earnings growth better than its competitors. We believe it remains well-positioned to scale revenues efficiently and deliver sustainable value as it continues reducing expenses and returning excess capital via share repurchases.

Health care products manufacturer Abbott Laboratories is executing well, capitalizing on its diverse mix of businesses to drive growth. In Q1, the company announced European approval (CE Mark) for its Volt Pulse Field Ablation (PFA) system, which is designed to provide a new therapy option for people battling heart rhythm disorders such as atrial fibrillation. SS&C Technologies provides software products and software-enabled services to financial services and health care companies. It has executed well in several of its businesses — a trend that is expected to continue and generate accelerating organic growth.

Among our bottom individual contributors in Q1 were Regal Rexnord (RRX) and lululemon athletica (LULU). Power transmission components manufacturer Regal Rexnord faced pressure amid growing tariff-related concerns. Generally, its organic growth has held up better than peers, and though its leverage is somewhat elevated, merger synergies and debt paydown rates are beating expectations — factors which should position it well relative to competitors. We believe the recent weakness has been an overreaction and maintain our conviction in our thesis.

Shares of athletic apparel manufacturer lululemon were pressured amid ongoing concerns about potential brand saturation in the Americas, its largest market. However, we believe the company is operating well and is positioned to improve US sales performance while continuing to grow international sales — factors which make the current valuation compelling, in our view.

Other bottom Q1 contributors included Cimpress (CMPR), Coherent (COHR) and Ashland (ASH). Custom printing services provider Cimpress’s US consumer segment faced softer holiday demand and higher marketing costs, weighing on results. Despite these near-term challenges, our thesis remains intact as the company continues shifting focus toward higher-value small business products with better customer retention and profitability.

Coherent is a global leader in materials, networking and lasers for the industrial, communications, electronics and instrumentation markets. Investor sentiment weighed on shares during the quarter — however, its datacom business continues outperforming, and the telecom cycle is showing signs of turning positive, signaling the potential for upside from here.

Shares of specialty chemical manufacturer Ashland have been pressured by dour sentiment, even as earnings have generally met expectations. The company has indicated that potential tariffs should not particularly affect it, given that most of its production locations and end markets are well- matched within countries. Regardless, investors seem relatively unconvinced, possibly also factoring in broader economic growth concerns.

Portfolio Activity

As volatility has picked up, we have been particularly watchful, preparing to reposition the portfolio for the period ahead as we find high-quality companies trading at particularly compelling valuations. In Q1, this resulted in one new position — Capital One (COF) — though we wouldn’t necessarily be surprised if portfolio activity picks up as volatility continues into Q2.

Capital One is one of the US’s largest credit card issuers and is known for having the industry’s best technology platform, which has helped drive improved operating efficiency and strong underwriting and helps in refreshing card offerings — a key to maintaining its position in a highly competitive industry. Capital One announced the acquisition of Discover in early 2024 — a deal that should unlock growth potential as it gets Capital One closer to merchants and gives the company access to the data necessary to create more competitive card offerings than peers. Though the merger faces a Department of Justice challenge, we believe the current share price fails to appreciate the near- and long-term synergies once the deal is approved, and accordingly, we capitalized on the attractive valuation to initiate a position in the quarter.

We funded this purchase in part with the proceeds from the sale of our position in internet retail and cloud infrastructure company Amazon (AMZN), whose shares had approached our estimate of intrinsic value. We also exited our positions in US-based mass retailer Target (TGT)and innovative medical technology company Enovis (ENOV) in favor of more compelling investing opportunities elsewhere.

Market Outlook

As we start Q2, uncertainty is as high as it has been, arguably since the pandemic’s earliest days. While markets struggle to digest the likeliest ramifications of the US’s newly announced tariffs — whether they are largely a negotiation tactic whose economic impact will moderate over time as countries and companies reach better terms with the US, or whether they are here to stay for the long term and we will indeed see a significant reshaping of the world trade order — we are watching several factors particularly closely. Foremost, we are monitoring the relationship between the US and China, which is front and center in this trade showdown. As we write, President Trump has threatened a 104% tariff on China in response to its retaliatory increase to 84%. Given the magnitude of the US and Chinese economies, seeing the two countries enter a full-blown trade war would have significant, widespread implications for many US companies, up and down the capitalization spectrum.

Further, the increased uncertainty across a range of macroeconomic policies — trade as well as domestic spending and the administration’s ongoing efforts to meaningfully shrink the size of the federal government — is prompting concerns about the consumer. Should tariffs prompt a significant spike in inflation and/or fiscal spending cuts go too far, consumers could rein in their spending.

Indeed, companies across various sectors have noted a weakening consumer, suggesting the economy is slowing. With the consumer powering a significant majority of the economy, further weakening may present a risk to the lofty double-digit corporate earnings growth the market had been expecting for 2025.

However, regardless of how uncomfortable such volatility can be, we are simultaneously encouraged by its impact on the valuations of some companies that we believe are particularly strong and well-positioned to weather the environment ahead — even if it requires further suffering before economic and market growth resume. Accordingly, we have sought to be active in this period, identifying high- quality companies with particularly solid balance sheets whose outlook over the next three to five years is positive in our view. As downward volatility has continued, we have further acted on our research efforts to introduce these names to the portfolio, with the aim of positioning the portfolio well for whatever the environment gives us in the period ahead — whether there is further downside to endure, or whether markets turn around precipitously. We believe, with hindsight, that this period may prove to be one that separates those investors with a rigorous, bottom-up process from those unwilling or unable to see through the near-term volatility to the possibilities beyond.

Our primary focus is always on achieving value-added results for our existing clients, and we believe we can achieve better-than-market returns over the next five years through active portfolio management.

data.title: Trump Is Pushing For A Rate Cut: Is Powell Really Too Late?
data.description:
Intro

President Trump has tried in every way to say that he would like a rate cut, but Powell (or more generally the FOMC) still seems reluctant at the moment. Actually, the more time passes, the less willing he seems to agree with Trump's opinion.

A month ago, the probability that the Fed would not cut rates in mid-June was 72.40%, while today it has risen to 95.60%. Yet unemployment is low, inflation is under control, and major US companies are posting record profits. The ECB has cut rates for eight meetings in a row (including today's cut), and Trump believes it is time for the Fed to follow suit. He has repeatedly called Powell “too slow,” but is that really the case?

The Fed is not known for cutting rates at the right time; on the contrary, it usually goes into panic-cutting mode during a recession. However, on this occasion, I believe Powell's approach is the right one.

The US has never taken such a clear stance on tariffs in recent history, and this could clearly have repercussions on inflation. For now, everything seems under control, but we need to wait a little longer to say with certainty that tariffs will not impact the inflation rate. Personally, I believe we will be able to draw conclusions at the end of the summer, so it is still too early. Consequently, I agree with the Fed's view and think it is better to wait a little longer before starting to cut rates.

Inflationary spirals do not occur overnight, or even from one month to the next; it takes time for them to spread throughout the economy. It starts with a few companies with inelastic demand raising their prices, then the phenomenon spreads on a large scale. Initially, the price increase is reflected only in products subject to tariffs, but then it extends to all others.

For a matter of credibility, the Fed cannot risk lowering rates now in the hope that tariffs will not generate inflation. Credibility is everything for a central bank, and the Fed cannot afford to send mixed signals about its monetary policy. Imagine a scenario in which inflation starts to rise in July/August and the Fed has already lowered rates: what does it do then? Raising rates again would be a clear signal that it has no idea what is going on. Better to make a few good moves than many confused ones.

The New York Fed recently released a business survey covering about 200 manufacturing and service companies in the New York-Northern New Jersey area. I find the results very interesting in understanding why the Fed is postponing rate cuts.

The New York Fed survey

The first important slide in this survey shows the number of companies that will pass on the increased costs due to tariffs to consumers.

As you can see from this image, approximately 38% of the companies surveyed passed on the entire cost of the tariffs to consumers; 24% absorbed the entire cost, accepting a reduction in profit margins. The extremes of this survey are significant: 62% of companies are either placing a heavy burden on consumers or on their profit margins. In my opinion, this result shows a clear disparity in how tariffs affect companies. Those with a strong competitive advantage have no problem passing them on to consumers; less solid businesses are becoming even weaker because they have to bear the full cost of the tariffs. In between, there is a well-distributed 38% that is trying to adopt a hybrid approach to overcome the current uncertainty.

In the coming months, I expect the distribution of data to be more skewed to the right. The 38% of companies that have chosen to increase prices by the amount of the tariffs are providing the initial trigger for inflation to rise. Companies that have not followed suit may do so as soon as they see their costs rise too much, triggering an inflationary spiral. Those who have not increased their prices may be forced to do so. Why should others raise their prices and I don't? That is the question they will ask themselves.

Clearly, the companies in this survey represent a small sample that is not representative of the entire US economy. The true value of tariff pass-through could be higher or lower than this. As far as I am concerned, I don't think it's too different, as I read every day about companies that have started to raise prices. Although we do not know the exact magnitude of the trend, we know that it is happening.

Let's now look at the second slide, which shows how quickly tariffs have led to price increases by companies.

58% increased prices within a month from the introduction of the tariffs; the remaining 42% took longer or are still considering a price increase. Based on the sample analyzed, it would appear that price increases due to tariffs are not yet widespread. Considering that in the 1-3 month sample there are companies that haven't raised prices yet (it hasn't been 3 months since Liberation Day), I'd say that about 50% of companies have already raised prices; the other half still haven't. Also, as we saw earlier, not all the companies that raised prices were able to pass on the tariffs entirely to consumers. Therefore, it is still too early to judge the effects of the tariffs. Companies are still thinking about the best strategy: once pre-tariff inventories have been exhausted, they will have to make a choice.

Finally, the last slide takes into account how companies' operations are changing due to the tariffs.

In this case, there are two interesting factors to note:

  • The first is that a quarter of companies are countering tariffs by increasing prices even for goods/services not related to them. This reflects a very common process during periods of inflation, as companies almost never limit themselves to increasing the price of individual products/services. The increase is widespread, as companies have to cope with higher costs due to factors such as wage and rent increases. In addition, about a third of companies are increasing inventory levels because they fear supply chain shocks or tariff increases.
  • The second factor that caught my attention is the deterioration of capital investment in the US by service companies; manufacturing companies are not faring much better. Trump sees tariffs as a way to get more companies to invest in the US, but for now, it's having the opposite effect. As for reducing imports, his plan is working, with almost a third cutting imports. But there's a problem. This has not been followed by an increase in imports from new non-US sources, so companies that import less have not found new trading partners to replace their previous ones. In other words, I expect those who have reduced their imports from China (the country most affected by tariffs) to have had difficulty finding another country to import from. The icing on the cake is that headcount is slightly down.

Companies seem to agree on one thing: tariffs are hurting the net income of many of them. This response was given by almost 50% of the companies surveyed and was the one with the clearest result. Of course, there are also companies that are not experiencing too many problems thanks to their competitive advantage (they can raise prices due to the inelastic demand for their products/services), but they can trigger an inflationary spiral and affect others.

Conclusion

Overall, the New York Fed survey highlights three interesting considerations:

  • Not all companies have started to raise prices.
  • Among the companies that are raising prices, there are also goods/services not directly related to tariffs.
  • Trump wanted tariffs to increase investment in the US, employment, and corporate profits; so far, this is not happening.

In light of all this, the Fed's decision not to cut rates yet seems quite reasonable, and we will probably only be able to judge the impact of tariffs on inflation in late summer; before then, it is too early to say. These concerns may prove unfounded, but the Fed cannot risk losing its credibility by acting a few months early. In a chaotic phase such as the current one, the market needs certainty, and a Fed that lowers and raises rates in a matter of months is far from encouraging; it is much better "to be late” by 1–2 months and doing the right choice.

A fiscal policy less inclined to increase the deficit would in itself be a way to reduce rates, as bond vigilantes would see US debt as safer and yields would fall. But it is much easier to blame the Fed.
